1: theme My heart is steadfast, O God! I will sing and make melody with all my being! Singing with all one's being — the wholehearted praise Christ models as the lead worshiper in the congregation (Hebrews 2:12; Psalms 22:22). Philippians 4:4: Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice. The steadfast heart is the heart kept by the peace of God (Philippians 4:7); the whole-being melody is the worship Christ enables and joins.
2: shadow Awake, O harp and lyre! I will awake the dawn! Awakening the dawn with praise — the resurrection morning. Christ is raised at dawn (Luke 24:1 — on the first day of the week, at early dawn), his resurrection awakening a new dawn for all creation. The harps find their eschatological fulfillment in Revelation 5:8: the four living creatures and elders hold harps and sing to the Lamb.
3: direct I will give thanks to you, O Lord, among the peoples; I will sing praises to you among the nations. Romans 15:9 includes this verse in Paul's catena supporting Gentile praise: Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles, and sing to your name. The psalmist's resolve to praise among the nations is fulfilled in Christ's mission (Matthew 28:19) and Paul's apostolate — the whole world now sings praises to the LORD through Christ.
4: revelation of God For your steadfast love is great above the heavens; your faithfulness reaches to the clouds. Love above the heavens and faithfulness to the clouds — the immeasurable love Paul reaches for in Ephesians 3:18-19: that you may have strength to comprehend... what is the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. The love that exceeds the heavens is the love of God revealed in Christ.
5: direct Be exalted, O God, above the heavens! Let your glory be over all the earth! God exalted above the heavens — fulfilled in Christ's exaltation: God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name (Philippians 2:9). His glory covers the earth: the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea (Habakkuk 2:14). The prayer of v5 is answered in the exaltation of the Son.
6: shadow That your beloved may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer me! The beloved delivered by God's right hand — Christ the beloved Son: This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased (Matthew 3:17). The beloved is delivered by the Father's right hand through resurrection (Acts 2:33: exalted at the right hand of God). Those in the beloved receive the same deliverance (Ephesians 1:6).
7: shadow God has promised in his holiness: "With exultation I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Vale of Succoth. God dividing the land in holy promise — the land of Canaan was the type; the inheritance Christ secures is the new heavens and new earth (Revelation 21:1). Hebrews 11:10: the patriarchs looked for a city whose builder and maker is God. God's holy promise of the land finds its ultimate fulfillment in the eternal inheritance (1 Peter 1:4).
8: shadow Gilead is mine; Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter. Judah as God's scepter — Genesis 49:10: the scepter shall not depart from Judah... until tribute comes to him. Christ, of the tribe of Judah (Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5 — the Lion of the tribe of Judah), is the fulfillment of the Judah-scepter: the ruler from Judah to whom the nations belong.
9: shadow Moab is my washbasin; upon Edom I cast my shoe; over Philistia I shout in triumph. The surrounding nations subdued — the universal lordship of Christ. Revelation 17:14: the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings. The enemies of Israel are types of the spiritual forces Christ overcomes at the cross: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Colossians 2:15).
10: shadow Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom? Asking who will lead to the fortified city — the question Christ answers. He is the way to the city no human strategy can reach (John 14:6). The forerunner who enters the inner place behind the curtain on our behalf (Hebrews 6:19-20) is the one who brings us to the fortified city no army can take by force.
11: shadow Have you not rejected us, O God? You do not go out, O God, with our armies. God seeming to abandon — the darkness of Gethsemane and Golgotha. Christ enters the experience of divine absence: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46) — so that God will never truly abandon those who are in him (Hebrews 13:5: I will never leave you nor forsake you). His cry purchased the promise of perpetual presence.
12: shadow Oh grant us help against the foe, for vain is the salvation of man. Human salvation is vain — the inadequacy of all human help drives the necessity of divine rescue. Salvation belongs to the LORD (Jonah 2:9). No human effort reaches the depth of need; only the divine-human Savior is sufficient. Acts 4:12: there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.
13: shadow With God we shall do valiantly; it is he who will tread down our foes. Valor and victory through God — the confidence of Romans 8:37: in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us. The courage to do valiantly is not human bravado but faith in the one who has already trodden down the ultimate foe: the last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Corinthians 15:26), and Christ destroys it in his resurrection.
